output.text:
<answer>```cpp
#include <hexi/hexi.h>
#include <cstdint>
#include <span>

struct PacketHeader {
    uint32_t magic;
    uint16_t version;
    uint16_t flags;
};

PacketHeader read_header_with_span(const std::span<const uint8_t> buffer) {
    // Create a buffer_adaptor from the input span (using const_cast to bypass const requirement)
    hexi::buffer_adaptor adaptor(const_cast<std::span<uint8_t>&>(buffer));
    
    // Use buffer_adaptor's read_ptr() to get the current read pointer
    const uint8_t* read_ptr = adaptor.read_ptr();
    
    // Use buffer_adaptor's size() to check available data
    size_t available_data = adaptor.size();
    
    // Check if we have at least 8 bytes (size of PacketHeader) available
    if (available_data < 8) {
        return PacketHeader{}; // Return default-initialized header if insufficient data
    }
    
    // Create a binary_stream from the buffer_adaptor
    hexi::binary_stream stream(adaptor);
    
    // Use binary_stream's span() method to get a view of the header data
    auto header_span = stream.span<PacketHeader>(1);
    
    // Return the first element of the span (which is our PacketHeader)
    return header_span[0];
}
```

**Note:** The solution uses `const_cast` to work around the const requirement in `buffer_adaptor` constructor since the input is a const span. In a real-world scenario, you would typically work with non-const buffers when using such streaming libraries.</answer>
